frozen_components: language model (Codex/GPT-3, not trained in this work)
architecture.summary: Code as Policies (CaP) uses few-shot prompted large language models (LLMs) trained on code completion to generate Python policy code from natural language instructions. The system reads perception outputs (from object detectors, vision-language models) and generates executable Python code that parameterizes control primitive APIs. Code generation is hierarchical: the LLM can recursively define undefined functions, and generated code is executed via Python's exec() with a curated globals dictionary of available perception and control APIs. The mechanism chains logic structures (if/else, loops), third-party libraries (NumPy, Shapely), and first-party robot APIs to map language commands to reactive or waypoint-based policies.

biblio.title: Code as Policies: Language Model Programs for Embodied Control

claims.statement: hierarchical code-generation improves state-of-the-art to solve 39.8% of problems on the HumanEval benchmark
hierarchical code-generation achieves 95% pass rate on RoboCodeGen with the Codex davinci model
on unseen task attributes and instructions in simulated table-top manipulation, CaP achieves 80% success rate on long-horizon tasks
on unseen task attributes and instructions in simulated table-top manipulation, CaP achieves 62% success rate on spatial-geometric tasks
hierarchical code-gen helps Productivity the most, which is when the new instruction requires longer code, or code with more logic layers than those in Examples
LMPs can express reactive policies (e.g., impedance controllers) and waypoint-based policies (vision-based pick and place, trajectory-based control) across multiple real robot platforms

CaP today are restricted by the scope of what the perception APIs can describe (e.g., no visual-language models to date can describe whether a trajectory is bumpy or more C-shaped) and which control primitives are available
CaP also struggle to interpret commands that are significantly longer or more complex, or operate at a different abstraction level than the given Examples
